petitioner_background: The petitioner is a self-represented inventor seeking classification as an alien of extraordinary ability under section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act. He filed a patent application with the USPTO for an invention (described as a machine) and is attempting to become an entrepreneur in the United States based on his invented machine. The petition was filed with the Texas Service Center, which denied it for failure to meet the initial evidence requirements.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted screenshots from two websites (names redacted, but described as sites covering patent applications). One site covers 'the entire collection of granted patents and published patent applications from the USPTO' and other patent offices. The other site posts 'the latest United States patent applications each week before the USPTO decides to grant or deny them.' The documentation included the petitioner's own patent application for his invention that he submitted to the USPTO.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found that the patent documentation 'does not discuss the petitioner, is not about the petitioner, and does not otherwise reflect published material about the petitioner relating to his work.' The plain language of 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(iii) requires published material to be about the petitioner and printed in professional or major trade publications or other major media with significant national or international distribution. The petitioner's submission of his own patent application does not reflect evidence of published material about him relating to his work.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: On appeal, the petitioner referred to USPTO Form 892, Notices of References Cited, which reflected eight references. The petitioner also submitted evidence that he filed a patent application with the USPTO that was currently being adjudicated. The petitioner described features of his invention and compared the old version of his patent to his current amendments.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO explained that while citation of a petitioner's work by others could demonstrate interest and significance, the Form 892 references were cited by the USPTO examiner during prosecution of the petitioner's patent application — they are not evidence that the petitioner's work has been cited by others. The petitioner submitted no documentary evidence that his invention has been cited by others in their own work. Citing Matter of New York State Department of Transportation, 22 I&N Dec. 215, 221 n.7, the AAO noted that even a granted patent is not necessarily evidence of a track record of success with influence over the field; a patent recognizes originality but does not demonstrate a contribution of major significance. The petitioner submitted no evidence that anyone is licensing or using the patented technology. Additionally, the AAO emphasized that the regulation's use of the plural 'contributions' requires more than one original contribution of major significance, and the petitioner had not shown even one.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: On appeal, the petitioner claimed that a website (name redacted) 'and several other websites published this; copy of which is unavailable as mentioned in annexure-33 of the RFE sent.' In the RFE response, the petitioner claimed another website 'also published my work in 2007 but copies of which are traceless now.' The petitioner's 'List of Attachments' indicated that annexure 33 was 'Missing.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found that the record of proceeding contains no evidence that the petitioner has authored scholarly articles on the referenced websites. Citing Matter of Soffici, 22 I&N Dec. 158, 165 (Comm'r 1998) and Matter of Treasure Craft of California, 14 I&N Dec. 190, the AAO held that 'going on record without supporting documentary evidence is not sufficient for purposes of meeting the burden of proof in these proceedings.'

outcome_reasoning: The AAO dismissed the appeal because the petitioner failed to satisfy the initial evidence requirements of 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3), having neither submitted evidence of a one-time achievement (a major, internationally recognized award) nor evidence meeting at least three of the ten regulatory criteria. The AAO also rejected the petitioner's request for consideration under the comparable evidence provision at 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(4), finding that the regulation does not allow comparable evidence where an alien is simply unable to meet or submit documentary evidence of three criteria, and that the petitioner failed to explain why the regulatory criteria were not readily applicable to his occupation as an inventor. Although the AAO noted it need not conduct a final merits determination under Kazarian, it stated that a review of the evidence in the aggregate supports a finding that the petitioner has not demonstrated the level of expertise required for the classification sought. The petitioner abandoned the criteria he did not contest on appeal.
legal_citations: Section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act
Rijal v. USCIS, 772 F.Supp.2d 1339 (W.D. Wash. 2011), aff'd, 683 F.3d 1030 (9th Cir. 2012)
Sepulveda v. U.S. Att'y Gen., 401 F.3d 1226, 1228 n.2 (11th Cir. 2005)
Silverman v. Eastrich Multiple Investor Fund, L.P., 51 F.3d 28, 31 (3rd Cir. 1995)
APWU v. Potter, 343 F.3d 619, 626 (2nd Cir. Sep 15, 2003)
Matter of New York State Department of Transportation, 22 I&N Dec. 215, 221 n.7 (Assoc. Comm'r 1998)
Maramjaya v. USCIS, Civ. Act. No. 06-2158 (RCL) (D.C. Cir. March 26, 2008)
Snapnames.com Inc. v. Chertoff, 2006 WL 3491005 (D. Or. Nov. 30, 2006)
Matter of Treasure Craft of California, 14 I&N Dec. 190 (Reg'l Comm'r 1972)
Soltane v. United States Dep't of Justice, 381 F.3d 143, 145 (3d Cir. 2004)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: The petitioner was self-represented and failed to identify which regulatory criteria his evidence purportedly met, either in the original filing or in response to the RFE. The petitioner's own 'List of Attachments' indicated that annexure 33, which he referenced as evidence of scholarly articles, was 'Missing' from the submission. The petitioner also admitted that copies of alleged 2007 publications of his work were 'traceless now.' The AAO found that the petitioner abandoned the criteria he did not contest on appeal (criteria (i)-(ii), (iv), and (vii)-(x)) under Sepulveda v. U.S. Att'y Gen. No fraud or misrepresentation findings were made.
